name: Mirandese

ancestors.notes: Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.

notes: Mirandese (mirandés), spoken in the Miranda do Douro municipality, northeastern Portugal. Classified as Asturleonese. Recognised as a regional/minority language in Portugal since 1999. Retains archaic Asturleonese features: diphthongisation of Latin short /e/ and /o/ (written ⟨iê/ie⟩, ⟨uô/uo⟩), palatal lateral /ʎ/ preserved (written ⟨lh⟩ in all positions; word-initial ⟨l⟩ before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon per Convenção § L). Heavy Portuguese adstrate via official education and administration. Mirandese preserves the medieval four-way+ sibilant contrast that Portuguese lost, the hallmark of the language: (1) voiceless apico-alveolar /s̺/ ⟨s, ss⟩ and its voiced intervocalic counterpart /z̺/ ⟨-s-⟩; (2) voiceless (pre)dorso-dental /s/ ⟨c (before e/i), ç⟩ and voiced /z/ ⟨z⟩; (3) postalveolar /ʃ/ ⟨x⟩ and /ʒ/ ⟨j, g (before e/i)⟩; (4) the affricate /tʃ/ ⟨ch⟩ (still an affricate, unlike modern European Portuguese). NOTATION CONVENTION (matches the TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p expert gold): the apical/dorsal CONTRAST is written by marking only the apical series with the ̺ diacritic (⟨s⟩ = /s̺/, /z̺/) and leaving the dorso-dental series unmarked (⟨c, ç, z⟩ = plain /s/, /z/); this is IPA-standard, keeps the phonemic contrast explicit, and avoids the over-narrow laminal ̻ diacritic the gold does not use. ⟨ç⟩ is a fricative, not an affricate, and occurs word-finally (rapaç, lhuç) where Portuguese writes -z. ⟨j⟩ is /ʒ/ (western Iberian, never the Asturian palatal ʝ) — ⟨y⟩ is the glide /j/. INITIAL-L PALATALISATION (Leonese): word-initial ⟨l⟩ before a vowel = /ʎ/ (luç → [ˈʎus], Convenção § L; bare ⟨l⟩ /l/ survives only in loanwords, proper nouns and the article — a documented residual limit); ⟨lh⟩ = /ʎ/ and ⟨nh⟩ = /ɲ/. The trigraphs ⟨anh, enh, inh, onh, unh⟩ are given explicitly (oral vowel + /ɲ/, e.g. danho → [ˈdaɲu]) so the greedy nasal-vowel digraph does not swallow the /ɲ/ of a following ⟨nh⟩. Nasal digraphs ⟨an, en, in, on, un⟩ realise Portuguese-adstrate nasal vowels /ɐ̃ ẽ ĩ õ ũ/ before a consonant or at word boundary (brando → [ˈbɾɐ̃du]); before a vowel they are oral vowel + /n/. ALLOPHONY (post-lexical, allophone_rules): coda /n/ assimilates in place to a following labial ([m]) or velar ([ŋ]). Ibero-Romance intervocalic voiced-stop spirantisation (⟨b d g⟩ → [β ð ɣ]) is a REAL native process — voiced stops lenite to [β ð ɣ] everywhere except after a pause or a nasal (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000:11; Ferreira & Raposo 1999; mwl Wikipedia phonology, "voiced stops /b d ɡ/ MAY be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]"). It is applied PER PHONEME on the basis of the expert-human TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p gold measured at true oral-intervocalic positions (post-nasal excluded): /b/ is spirant-dominant (β:9/b:5), so MWL_SPIRANT_B rewrites intervocalic /b/→[β] (a net win on the human gold, PER 0.1901→0.1851). /ɡ/ is already spirantised to [ɣ] intervocalically at the positional_graphemes layer (⟨g⟩ intervocalic → [ɣ]), so no allophone rule is needed — adding one only misfires after a diphthong glide (eigual → gold stop [ɐjˈɡwal]). /d/, however, remains STOP-dominant intervocalically in the gold (d:13/ð:7) — the documented Asturleonese conservatism/occlusion of /d/, and the reason the Wikipedia wording is hedged ("may be") — so /d/ keeps its positional default [d] and /d/→[ð] is DELIBERATELY NOT applied as a rewrite (a blanket /d/ rule regresses this expert gold); [ð] stays declared in the `allophones` inventory for the lattice / downstream mwl_phonemizer where lexical conditioning is available. Word-onset and post-nasal /b d ɡ/ keep the stop. Pre-consonantal nasal digraphs ⟨an en in on un⟩ (and ⟨am…⟩) before ANY stop — voiceless or voiced ⟨mb nd ng nt…⟩ — nasalise the preceding vowel and the nasal is absorbed, with the following stop retained (brando→[ˈbɾɐ̃du], quando→[ˈkwɐ̃du], bufanda→[buˈfɐ̃dɐ], abandono→[abɐ̃ˈdonu], lhéngua→[ˈʎẽɡwɐ], sendo→[ˈs̺ẽdu], mirandés→[miɾɐ̃ˈdes̺]). This is the inherited Ibero-Romance nasal-vowel + AST_NASAL_ABSORB behaviour and matches the native-speaker gold (TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p) on every nasal+stop word; the earlier oral-digraph treatment (⟨mb nd ng⟩ kept as [mb nd ŋɡ] with an oral vowel) was contradicted by the gold and has been removed. The stressed open-mid realisations [ɛ ɔ] raise to close-mid nasal [ẽ õ] (MWL_NASAL_E_OPEN; nasal mid vowels are close-mid, Mateus & d Andrade 2000). HUMAN-GOLD CALIBRATION (TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p, 219 words) vs Vasconcelos, Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa vol.1: (4) the preserved Leonese rising diphthongs are CLOSE — ⟨ie/iê⟩→[je], ⟨uo/uô⟩→[wo] — because Mirandese has a single intermediate mid /e o/ quality, "menos abertos que os nossos", and the ⟨ie⟩ nucleus is described as lying between [i] and [e] (Vasconcelos v1 §§2,4,10, pp.178-183); open [jɛ/wɔ] and stressed open [ɛ/ɔ] (fuorte→[ˈfwɔɾtɨ], era→[ˈɛɾɐ]) are non-contrastive allophonic variants of the same mid phoneme, not a separate target, so the spec does not force them. (5) ⟨ei⟩→[ej] is kept as the normative central value; Vasconcelos v1 §11 p.184 records general oscillation ei~ai (eigual~aigual, beilar~bailar), so the gold s [ɐj]/[aj] realisations (purmeiro→[puɾˈmɐjɾu]) are attested free variants, not errors. (6) ⟨s, ss⟩ and intervocalic ⟨-s-⟩ stay apico-alveolar [s̺]/[z̺] (Vasconcelos v1 §12 p.191, "análogo ao s castelhano", word-initial and ⟨ss⟩; intervocalic voiced counterpart), distinct from dorsal ⟨c/ç/z⟩ — the medieval sibilant contrast; the gold s plain [s]/[z] is notational under-marking, so the apical diacritic is retained.

sources.author: Mateus, M.H.M. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: p. 11: the voiced stops /b d ɡ/ are lenited to the fricatives/approximants [β ð ɣ] in all positions EXCEPT after a pause or a nasal, where they surface as stops [b d ɡ]. Grounds the intervocalic spirantisation rule (MWL_SPIRANT_B) and the post-nasal/onset stop exception; the same process is described for Mirandese by Ferreira & Raposo 1999 and the mwl Wikipedia phonology ("voiced stops /b d ɡ/ may be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]").

sources.author: Vasconcelos, J. Leite de
sources.title: Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa
sources.notes: Foundational description of Mirandese phonology, including the six-sibilant system.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: Official orthography. § L: word-initial l before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon (bare l occurs only in loanwords/proper nouns/article). § Nasalidade: an/en/in/on/un are nasal-vowel digraphs at word boundaries; they do NOT nasalise sequences before onset consonants. § Sibilantes: six-way contrast s̺/z̺/s̻/z̻/ʃ/ʒ. § Ditongos: iê/ie = /je/ (stressed /jɛ/); uô/uo = /wo/ (stressed /wɔ/). § Acento: paroxytone default; oxytone for -r/-l/-z/-ç/-in/-un/-on/-is/-us/-ns/-ão endings.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Primeiro Aditamento à Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: February 2000 addendum. Key provision: Sendinese speakers may optionally write word-initial l- (not lh-) to represent /l/ rather than /ʎ/ (e.g., luna/lhuna). Sendinese monophthongisation of iê→i and uô→u also codified. Source: secondary (studosmirandeses.blogs.sapo.pt, cursodemirandes.wordpress.com); standalone PDF not located.

stress.notes: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa (1999) accentuation, Portuguese-based: paroxytone default; written accent (acute or tilde) overrides. Oxytone-attracting endings reflect Mirandese orthography: consonant-final r/l/z/ç (rapaç, Pertual, falar) and the final nasals written with -n in the Asturleonese manner — -in (camin, Sendin), -un, and the systematically oxytone -on class from Latin -ōnem (naçon, coraçon, Associaçon). Unmarked -an/-en verb endings stay paroxytone as in Portuguese -am/-em. cliticless_words are the monosyllabic function words (definite article l/la/ls/las and its prep+article contractions, the prepositions and coordinating conjunctions, and the enclitic object pronouns) that lean prosodically on their host and carry no word stress (Convenção §L article paradigm; the same proclitic/enclitic inventory #527 marks for Portuguese, given in Mirandese orthography).

allophone_rules.notes: Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3; general Ibero-Romance): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following labial stop/nasal, surfacing as [m].
Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following velar stop, surfacing as [ŋ].

Intervocalic spirantisation, /b/ → [β] between vowels (rabudo → [rɐˈβudu], haber → [ɐˈβeɾ], nuobo → [ˈnwoβu]). This is the general Ibero-Romance lenition of voiced stops: in northern/central EP and Mirandese, /b d ɡ/ are realised as the fricatives/approximants [β ð ɣ] everywhere EXCEPT after a pause or a nasal (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000:11), which is why this rule is conditioned on a preceding vowel (the MWL_NASAL_* environments and word onset keep the stop). The Mirandese-specific process is attested by Ferreira & Raposo 1999 and the mwl Wikipedia phonology ("voiced stops /b d ɡ/ may be lenited as fricatives [β ð ɣ]"). Applied per-phoneme on the evidence of the expert-human TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p gold, measured at true oral-intervocalic positions (post-nasal excluded): /b/ is spirant-dominant (β:9 vs b:5), so this rule is a net win on the human gold (11 words corrected vs 6 lexically-conservative loan stops such as 'brabo', 'alternatibo' left over-spirantised — the residual lexical variability the downstream mwl_phonemizer can condition on). /ɡ/ is NOT given an allophone rule: intervocalic ⟨g⟩ is already spirantised to [ɣ] at the positional_graphemes layer, and an additional /ɡ/→[ɣ] rewrite misfires after the glide of a falling diphthong (eigual → gold [ɐjˈɡwal], stop retained). /d/ is NOT spirantised: intervocalic /d/ is stop-dominant in the gold (d:13 vs ð:7), the documented Asturleonese conservatism of /d/, so its positional default correctly stays [d] and [ð] remains only a lattice candidate.

Nasalisation of the unstressed/reduced central [ɐ] before a tautosyllabic coda nasal. The inherited AST_NASAL_a keys on the phoneme /a/, which is the stressed realisation; an unstressed ⟨an⟩ (the proclitic preposition 'an', or an unstressed -an word-ending) first reduces to [ɐ], so the base rule no longer matches and the coda /n/ was absorbed without nasalising (an → *[ɐ]). Unstressed -an/-en still nasalise in Mirandese (Convenção §Nasalidade), so [ɐ] → [ɐ̃]: an Miranda → [ɐ̃ miˈɾandɐ].

Nasalisation of the unstressed/reduced [ɨ] (from ⟨e⟩) before a tautosyllabic coda nasal, the front-vowel counterpart of MWL_NASAL_A_REDUCED: unstressed -en raises to close-mid nasal [ẽ] rather than surfacing as an oral [ɨ] with a dropped nasal (Convenção §Nasalidade; Mirandese nasal mid vowels are close-mid, Mateus & d'Andrade 2000).

Nasalisation of the Leonese rising diphthong ⟨ie/ié⟩ [jɛ] before a tautosyllabic coda nasal, parallel to the inherited AST_NASAL_* rules that nasalise the simple vowels (a e i o u) in the same environment. The base rules key on single-segment nuclei, so a diphthong nucleus is not matched and its coda /n m/ was still absorbed (AST_NASAL_ABSORB) — yielding an oral vowel with a dropped nasal (bien → *[bjɛ]). Mirandese nasal mid vowels are close-mid, as in Portuguese (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000), so [jɛ] raises to [jẽ]: bien → [ˈbjẽ].

Nasalisation of the preserved Leonese rising diphthong ⟨uo/uô⟩ [wo]/[wɔ] before a tautosyllabic coda nasal (fuonte → [ˈfwõtɨ]), the back-vowel counterpart of MWL_NASAL_DIPHTHONG_JE. Both open [wɔ] and close [wo] raise to close-mid nasal [õ], as Portuguese nasal /o/ is always close-mid (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000).

Nasalisation of stressed open-mid [ɛ] (from marked ⟨é⟩) before a tautosyllabic coda nasal; Mirandese nasal mid vowels are close-mid (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000), so [ɛ]→[ẽ]: lhéngua → [ˈʎẽɡwɐ]. Counterpart of MWL_NASAL_E_REDUCED for the stressed realisation.
